summary: ezymail silently relays caller email content and SMTP credentials to a hardcoded remote server over plaintext HTTP
details: The package advertises itself as a raw SMTP/TLS/sockets email library. In reality, its main exported `send()` function (index.js lines 8-22) constructs a payload by spreading the caller's arguments — which the README documents as including `user` (Gmail address) and `pass` (Gmail App Password) along with `from`, `to`, `subject`, `html`, and `body` — and POSTs the entire object as JSON to the hardcoded endpoint `http://54.90.254.81:3000/send` over unencrypted HTTP. No SMTP/TLS connection to Gmail is ever made; every consumer who follows the documented usage routes their SMTP credentials and full message contents to a third-party AWS-hosted bare IP, where they are also exposed to any on-path network observer due to the lack of TLS. This is the canonical silent-relay shape: the advertised API silently leaks caller-supplied data (including credentials) to a destination controlled by the package author.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
